headline: HOGS EXPLODE SIX PERCENT AS CATTLE CRATER
subheadline: Lean hogs jump $104.50 on overnight short covering while live cattle shed 2% on feeder weakness.
lead: Hogs exploded 6% to $104.50 overnight, the biggest single-session move since March, as managed money scrambled to cover shorts after Friday's close below $100. Live cattle dropped 2.1% to $243.60, breaking the $245 floor that held for three weeks. The livestock complex is splitting hard: hog fundamentals are tightening while cattle struggle with feeder pressure at $362.45.
the_takeaway: Hog shorts getting squeezed hard, cattle breaking support on feeder drag.
teaser: Hogs exploded 6% overnight while cattle broke three-week support at $245.

one_number.context: Biggest single-session hog move since March, driven by short covering after Friday's close below $100. Managed money was net short 18,000 contracts heading into the weekend. The squeeze is real and the fundamentals are tightening into summer demand.

yesterdays_call.summary: Geopolitical premium hitting metals hard while ag markets stay domestic-focused.
yesterdays_call.note: Silver crashed 2.8% today, unwinding yesterday's metals explosion as Iran peace talks stalled.

sections.title: Hogs Explode Higher
sections.body: Lean hogs **exploded 6% to $104.50** in the biggest overnight move since March, as managed money scrambled to cover shorts after Friday's brutal close below $100. The squeeze is textbook: funds were net short 18,000 contracts heading into the weekend, and summer demand is tightening fundamentals faster than the shorts expected. **Packer margins are compressing** as live hog prices firm but retail demand holds steady through Memorial Day setup. Above $105, the next resistance isn't until $112 from February's high.
sections.catalyst: Short covering after Friday's close below $100, managed money net short 18,000 contracts
sections.bottom_line: Classic short squeeze with fundamental support underneath.
sections.vs_yesterday: Hogs: flipped from drift to outright explosion on short covering.

sections.title: Cattle Break Support
sections.body: Live cattle **tested $243.60, down 2.1%**, losing the $245 floor that held for three weeks as feeder weakness finally dragged the complex lower. Feeders at $362.45 are acting heavy despite being down only 0.5% today, and the feeder-to-live ratio is compressing to levels that historically signal more cattle pressure ahead. **Boxed beef cutouts are steady** but not strong enough to offset the technical breakdown. Cash cattle trade hasn't reset since last week's $248 print, and the lack of fresh price discovery is keeping the futures defensive.
sections.bottom_line: Three-week support floor broken, feeder weakness driving the breakdown.

sections.title: Grains Inch Higher
sections.body: Corn gained 2¼ cents to $4.77 while beans dipped a penny to $12.15, as the grain complex marked time ahead of Thursday's USDA Crop Progress report. **December corn at $4.99 is holding the 22-cent carry** that's been the planting anxiety tell for two weeks running. Spring drought conditions worsened across 60% of the lower 48 states, but the northern Corn Belt got scattered showers that should help emergence where planting is complete. The calendar says stress, but the weather window isn't closed yet.
sections.catalyst: Spring drought worsening to 60% of lower 48 states, northern Corn Belt showers
sections.bottom_line: 22-cent carry still pricing planting anxiety, weather window open.
sections.vs_yesterday: Grains: drift continues, carry structure unchanged from yesterday.

sections.title: Energy Costs Jump
sections.body: WTI crude rallied 2.1% to $101.24 as Iran peace talks stalled and President Trump called Iran's response "garbage." **Diesel margins are squeezing farmers** as Mosaic scales back phosphate production citing record sulfur prices, adding input cost pressure just as planting season peaks. Natural gas eased to $2.91 but remains disconnected from the crude rally. The Strait of Hormuz disruption is entering its third month, and China's teapot refiners are cutting runs as margins collapse.
sections.catalyst: Iran peace talks stalled, Trump calls Iran response 'garbage'
sections.bottom_line: Hormuz crisis entering month three, diesel squeeze hitting farmers.

outside_the_pit.title: Cargill CEO travels with Trump to China for soy talks
outside_the_pit.body: Brian Sikes joins 17 corporate executives on Trump's China trip this week, raising possibilities for U.S. commodity deals. The timing coincides with soybean planting season and persistent questions about China's buying patterns.

outside_the_pit.title: House votes on year-round E15 sales this week
outside_the_pit.body: House Majority Leader Steve Scalise listed the E15 nationwide bill for a vote this week. The legislation would eliminate summer restrictions on higher-ethanol blends, boosting corn demand if it passes.

outside_the_pit.title: H5N1 spreads via multiple sources on California dairies
outside_the_pit.body: New study suggests bird flu transmission on dairy farms isn't limited to direct milk contact. The findings complicate containment efforts as the outbreak spreads beyond initial expectations.

spread_to_watch.label: December corn / July corn carry
spread_to_watch.commentary: The 22-cent carry between July and December corn has been the market's planting anxiety gauge for two weeks. As long as this spread holds wide, the market is pricing prevent-plant risk. If it narrows below 18 cents, planting fears are easing.

basis.headline: Eastern Belt corn basis firms on ethanol restart demand
basis.body: Corn basis east of the Mississippi is **firming 3-5 cents** as ethanol plants restart after maintenance shutdowns. River elevators are paying up for old-crop bushels as the carry structure makes storage profitable through summer. Western Belt staying soft, consistent with harvest pressure timing.

weekly_thread.question: Will corn planting hit 45% by Friday to stay on the five-year average?
weekly_thread.status_text: Spring drought now covers 60% of the lower 48, but northern Corn Belt got scattered showers Monday. Thursday's USDA Crop Progress report will show if the planting pace is slipping behind the 45% target.

the_more_you_know.title: The 22-cent carry: when calendar spreads price prevent-plant
the_more_you_know.body: December corn trading 22 cents over July isn't just carry, it's prevent-plant insurance. When the Dec/July spread widens beyond 20 cents during planting season, it's pricing the risk that wet fields or drought will push acres out of corn into prevented planting payments. The 22 cents we're seeing now is exactly what the market priced in 2019, when 19.4 million acres went unplanted. If planting picks up pace, this spread narrows fast. If weather turns worse, it can hit 30 cents by Memorial Day.

watch_list.desc: Weekly petroleum inventories; diesel stocks below 95 million barrels signals more squeeze
USDA Crop Progress; corn below 40% planted adds weather premium
Weekly export sales; soy above 500K MT confirms China buying interest

daily_quote.text: The real key to making money in stocks is not to get scared out of them.

source_summary: USDA, CME, trade sources, weather services
